name: Barcelona: Secrets of the Gothic Quarter

stops.name: Plaça de Catalunya
stops.shortDescription: A large plaza considered to be the exact center of Barcelona, separating the old city from the 19th-century Eixample district. It is famous for its beautiful fountains, sculptures, and flocks of pigeons.

stops.name: Cathedral of Barcelona
stops.shortDescription: A stunning Gothic cathedral constructed between the 13th and 15th centuries. Its cloister is famously home to exactly 13 white geese, honoring the age of the co-patron saint, Eulalia, when she was martyred.

stops.name: Pont del Bisbe
stops.shortDescription: A picturesque neo-Gothic bridge connecting two buildings over a narrow cobblestone street. Despite looking ancient, it was actually constructed in 1928 for the Barcelona International Exposition.

stops.name: Plaça de Sant Jaume
stops.shortDescription: The political heart of Barcelona, housing both the City Hall and the Palace of the Generalitat de Catalunya. It sits on the exact site of the ancient Roman Forum of Barcino.

stops.name: Plaça del Rei
stops.shortDescription: A majestic medieval public square bordered by the Palau Reial Major, the former residence of the Catalan counts. It is widely believed to be the place where King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella welcomed Columbus after his first voyage.

stops.name: Basilica of Santa Maria del Mar
stops.shortDescription: An outstanding example of Catalan Gothic architecture, built incredibly quickly in just 54 years during the 14th century. Its immense, unadorned stone interior gives it a feeling of vast, breathtaking space.

stops.name: Fossar de les Moreres
stops.shortDescription: A memorial plaza directly adjacent to the Basilica, built over a cemetery where defenders of the city were buried following the devastating Siege of Barcelona in 1714.

stops.name: El Born Centre de Cultura i Memòria
stops.shortDescription: A former public market building from the 19th century that now houses the expansive archaeological ruins of the medieval city beneath its massive iron and glass roof.

stops.name: Palau de la Música Catalana
stops.shortDescription: An architectural jewel of Catalan Modernism designed by Lluís Domènech i Montaner. Its stunning stained-glass skylight makes it one of the most beautiful concert halls in the world.

stops.name: Plaça de Sant Felip Neri
stops.shortDescription: A quiet, romantic, and tragic little square hidden deep in the Gothic Quarter. The church walls still bear the deep scars of shrapnel from a bombing during the Spanish Civil War.

stops.name: Mercat de la Boqueria
stops.shortDescription: Barcelona's most famous and vibrant public market, offering an incredible explosion of colors, smells, and tastes with its fresh fruit, jamón ibérico, and seafood stalls.

stops.name: La Rambla
stops.shortDescription: The city's most famous tree-lined pedestrian boulevard. It acts as the vibrant spine of the old city, constantly buzzing with street performers, tourists, and locals.

stops.name: Plaça Reial
stops.shortDescription: A lively, elegant square just off La Rambla, famous for its tall palm trees, bustling outdoor cafes, and unique street lamps designed by a young Antoni Gaudí.

stops.name: Palau Güell
stops.shortDescription: One of Gaudí's earliest masterpieces, a magnificent urban mansion designed for industrialist Eusebi Güell. It features incredibly ornate ironwork and a colorful rooftop.

stops.name: Mirador de Colom (Columbus Monument)
stops.shortDescription: Standing tall at the southern end of La Rambla near the port, this monument commemorates Christopher Columbus's return to Spain after his first voyage to the Americas.
